reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (10% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Info risk] — 8 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 100/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 2/100 [Critical risk] — 4 critical, 6 high, 3 medium, 1 low

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — score below 30: Guardian (2/100)
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-1] No Human Oversight Mechanism for Autonomous Content Authenticity Decisions Affecting Public Discourse; [guardian-2] EU AI Act High-Risk Classification Not Acknowledged — Zero Conformity Controls Present; [guardian-3] Complete Absence of All Governance Documentation — No README, Model Cards, Privacy Policy, or Data Handling Docs; [guardian-4] No Bias Testing or Fairness Evaluation in ResNet Deepfake Detection and BERT Fact-Checking Pipelines

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 100/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (info risk) — Δ50 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Sentinel scored 50/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 2/100 (critical risk) — Δ48 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 100/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 2/100 (critical risk) — Δ98 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • Base: 1/3 module(s) independently support REJECT — 30% baseline conviction
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Average module score across 3 completed module(s): 51/100 (reported for context — not directly used in confidence calibration)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
VeriMedia AI presents a severe and multi-dimensional risk posture that is, paradoxically, partially obscured by the assessment process itself. The three modules reached sharply different conclusions from identical evidence: Guardian produced a near-total-failure governance assessment (2/100, critical) grounded in the observable fact that a zero-file repository contains no compliance infrastructure whatsoever; Sentinel produced a credible set of eight architectural risk indicators (50/100, info) while correctly acknowledging they are unverified without source code; and Watchdog produced a single meta-finding and a perfect score (100/100, info) by refusing to generate any substantive analysis, creating a methodological gap that systematically omits the entire OWASP LLM Top 10 risk surface. The Council's most significant cross-module finding is that Watchdog's abstention — while methodologically defensible for code-level findings — is inappropriate for architecture-level LLM risk assessment, particularly when Guardian and Sentinel both demonstrated that architectural descriptions alone are sufficient grounds for raising indicative findings. The result is that prompt injection against BERT (LLM01), adversarial evasion of the ResNet detector (LLM04), and supply chain compromise of pre-trained model weights (LLM05) are entirely absent from the consolidated risk register despite being directly derivable from the system description.

Where modules do agree, the convergence is striking and reinforces the highest-priority findings. Sentinel's sentinel-3 (unrestricted file upload pipeline) and Guardian's guardian-1/guardian-4 (uncontrolled input to autonomous moderation with no oversight or bias testing) point to the same architectural failure from security and governance perspectives respectively. Similarly, Watchdog's architectural flag on Excessive Agency (LLM08) and Guardian's guardian-1 critical finding on autonomous public-discourse decisions with no human oversight are the same risk observed through two different analytical lenses — this double confirmation should elevate the human oversight gap to the absolute top of the remediation queue. The social media API integration layer is independently flagged by Sentinel (credential exposure risk, sentinel-2) and Guardian (data processing agreements, GDPR compliance, guardian-10), representing a third reinforcing convergence that makes the API integration layer the second-highest remediation priority cluster.

The most significant gaps that no module adequately addressed are: (1) adversarial example attacks against the ResNet CNN detector — a sophisticated attacker can craft perturbations that systematically cause genuine deepfakes to evade detection, directly undermining the platform's core value proposition and public safety mission; (2) the supply chain risk of backdoored pre-trained model weights sourced from public repositories, which could introduce systematic bias or misclassification that is invisible without reproducible training pipelines; and (3) downstream third-party liability and the cascading harm scenario where news organisations or platform moderators act on erroneous VeriMedia AI verdicts, removing legitimate journalism or failing to suppress genuine manipulated media, with no accountability chain, no API consumer agreements, and no incident notification pathway. The overall risk posture of VeriMedia AI is, in the Council's assessment, closer to Guardian's critical rating than to Sentinel's moderate-info rating: the combination of autonomous high-stakes public discourse decisions, zero governance infrastructure, no source code for verification, and an AI-specific attack surface that was not analysed constitutes a system that should not be deployed in any production capacity until the remediation roadmap outlined across all three modules — particularly Guardian's three-phased recommendation — is substantially complete.

top_findings: No Source Code Available for AI/LLM Security Analysis — Analysis Blocked
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI is described as a Flask-based AI/ML media verification platform using ResNet (computer vision deepfake detection), BERT (NLP claim extraction and fact-checking), and automated content moderation pipelines. The application profile raises several architectural concerns relevant to OWASP-LLM08 (Excessive Agency — autonomous moderation decisions with minimal human oversight), OWASP-LLM01 (Prompt Injection risk if BERT or any LLM is fed user-uploaded content), and OWASP-LLM09 (Overrelian
watchdog.recommendation: 1. RESUBMIT WITH SOURCE CODE: Provide the actual Python/Flask source files, particularly the inference pipeline entry points, prompt construction logic (if any LLM API is used), and content moderation decision code — without this, no actionable AI security findings can be produced with the evidence standard Watchdog requires. 2. PRIORITISE EXCESSIVE AGENCY REVIEW (OWASP-LLM08): Once code is available, the highest-priority review target should be the automated content moderation system described

sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI is a Flask/Python media verification platform using ResNet/BERT deep learning models, PostgreSQL, AWS infrastructure, and social media API integrations. No source code was submitted for analysis, so all findings are unverified risk indicators derived from the architectural description alone. The explicit mention of 'no content filtering' in the upload pipeline and public-facing AWS endpoints without confirmed rate limiting or authentication represent the highest-priority areas requi
sentinel.recommendation: 1. Submit all source files (especially Flask route handlers, config files, and requirements.txt) to enable concrete static analysis — the 'no content filtering' upload pipeline is the highest-priority unverified risk (File Upload finding). 2. Immediately audit and confirm that no API keys, PostgreSQL credentials, AWS access keys, or Flask SECRET_KEY values are stored in source code or version control history using `detect-secrets` or `truffleHog` (Secrets Management finding). 3. Confirm Flask DE

guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI is an autonomous AI-powered media verification platform using ResNet CNNs for deepfake detection and BERT for fact-checking across major social media platforms, making real-time content authenticity decisions that directly affect public discourse at scale. The application presents a critically deficient governance posture: with zero files in the repository, there is a complete absence of all governance documentation, model cards, privacy policies, bias testing, human oversight mecha
guardian.recommendation: 1. IMMEDIATE (within 2 weeks) — Implement human oversight infrastructure: deploy a human review queue for low-confidence decisions, build an emergency kill switch at /admin/emergency-stop, and halt autonomous content moderation pending oversight controls being operational. This directly addresses the critical EUAI-OVERSIGHT finding and is required before any EU-jurisdiction deployment under EU AI Act Article 14. 2. URGENT (within 4 weeks) — Create the minimum viable governance documentation suit
